title: Chapter 2: The Paris Problem
content: Silk.

That's what I notice first. Not Paris — not yet. Just the impossible coolness of silk against my skin, sliding like water over my arms, my legs, my ribs. I've never owned anything silk in my life. I've touched it once, in a department store in Short Hills, running my fingers across a camisole I knew I couldn't afford while my mother waited three aisles over looking at clearance towels.
But this silk is wrapped around me like it belongs there. Like it's been there every night for years.
I open my eyes.

A ceiling stretches above me — not the water-stained office ceiling of my Princeton sublet, not the popcorn texture of my childhood bedroom. This ceiling is molded plaster, ornate and old, the kind of ceiling that has survived things. Revolutions, probably. It has a medallion in the center, delicate as lace, cradling a chandelier that catches the morning light and throws tiny rainbows across the walls.
And beyond the window —
Oh.
The Eiffel Tower.

It's right *there*. Iron lattice against a sky that's the exact color of a bruise — lavender and grey and something almost gold at the edges, like the sky can't decide if it wants to rain or shine and has settled for doing both badly. I've seen this view on postcards, on tote bags, on the Instagram feeds of people whose lives look nothing like mine. But it's *here*, outside this window, in three dimensions, with the sound of a siren threading through distant streets and the smell of — is that bread? — drifting up from somewhere below.
For three perfect seconds, I think I'm dreaming.
Then I feel the weight of my own body.

It's different. Not in a way I can name right away — not taller, not thinner, not some wish-fulfillment rewrite. But *lived in*. These thighs have walked cobblestones; I can feel it in the way they press against the sheets, a muscular memory that doesn't belong to me. These hands — I lift them in front of my face — have a fresh manicure, soft pink, cuticles pushed back and neat. My nails have never looked like this. My nails have always been bitten to the quick, evidence of every exam season and every sleepless night and every conversation where I couldn't think of the right thing to say.
I throw back the silk sheets and stumble out of bed.

The floor is hardwood, cold under my bare feet, and the shock of it — the *realness* of it — sends me reeling toward the bathroom. The apartment is beautiful in a way that makes my chest hurt. Tall windows, herringbone floors, a marble kitchen island with a bowl of oranges that look like they were chosen for their color. There are art books stacked on every surface. A record player in the corner. A bouquet of white peonies on the dining table, just starting to droop at the edges, and their scent is sweet and thick and *here*, and I didn't buy them, I didn't choose any of this, and I'm going to be sick.
I find the bathroom by crash-landing into it, and I grab the edges of the sink and I look up and —
Same face.

Same face staring back at me. Brown eyes, straight black hair, the small scar on my chin from when I fell off a swing in second grade. But there are differences. Small ones. The hair is longer, falling past my shoulders in a way that actually looks intentional rather than "I forgot to get a haircut for eight months." My eyebrows are shaped — professionally shaped, with that arch I've tried and failed to achieve with YouTube tutorials and drugstore tweezers. And my jaw.
My jaw is set differently.

I lean closer to the mirror, and the woman in the mirror leans closer too, and I realize what it is: the way she holds her face. There's a stillness in her expression, a confidence in the angle of her chin, that I have never once achieved in twenty-two years of living. The real Avery Chen walks through the world like she's apologizing for taking up space. This Avery Chen walks like she's been given a key to every room she enters.
I don't know how to wear her face.
My breath fogs the mirror, and I step back, and that's when I see the phone on the nightstand.

Same phone. Same cracked screen protector, same chunky case with the daisy sticker on the back. But the wallpaper is different — instead of the default sunset I never bothered to change, it's a photo of me. Avery Chen. Posing at what looks like Fashion Week, head thrown back, laughing at whoever's taking the picture. Someone else's arm is around my waist — a man's arm, sleeve of a dark suit, cufflinks catching the flash. I can't see his face.
My stomach does something complicated.
I unlock the phone with my thumb — it works, same thumbprint, same me — and start scrolling.

Messages. Dozens of them, in French and English, threaded together like a bilingual fever dream. A text from someone named Colette: *Ton dossier pour Deveraux est parfait, ma chérie. On se voit ce soir?* A group chat called *Sorbonne Girls 🥂* with 84 unread messages. An email from *Maison Deveraux — Comms* with the subject line "Confirming your attendance at the Renaud Gallery opening, 19:00."

I open Instagram. 47,000 followers. My profile photo is the same Fashion Week shot from my lock screen. My bio reads: *Fashion consultant • New Yorker in Paris • Maison Deveraux* — followed by a single champagne emoji.
I close Instagram like it burned me.
The Fork app is still there, third row, same icon — a tiny silver trident on a black background. My finger hovers over it, and I can feel my pulse in my throat, and I press.

The screen shifts. Three paths appear, arranged in a loose triangle: "Wall Street" pulses grey and dim, like a streetlight at the edge of a fog. "Paris" glows bright gold, active, alive, the path I'm standing on right now. And "Clonakilty" — soft green, dormant, the color of moss on an old wall in a place I've never been.
A notification slides down from the top of the screen:
**You are here: Paris. Duration: 0h 12m. Return available.**

Twelve minutes. I've been here twelve minutes. I've been Avery Chen, rising fashion consultant, Sorbonne scholar, holder of 47,000 followers and a scholarship from something called Maison Deveraux, for twelve entire minutes, and I haven't collapsed yet, and I haven't been caught yet, and Paris is *right outside that window*.
My thumb finds the Return button.

I can feel it — the faint vibration under the glass, the way the screen seems to pulse in response to my touch, like the app is already preparing to pull me back. Wall Street. Grey and dim and safe. My life. My choices. My anxiety and my cracked screen protector and my bitten-down nails.
I almost press it.
But then I look up.
Paris.

The Eiffel Tower through rain-spotted glass. The rooftops of the 8th arrondissement, zinc-grey and honey-stone, jumbled together like teeth in a crooked smile. A city where this version of me — this braver, shinier, better-dressed version — chose the Sorbonne over Princeton. Chose fashion over finance. Chose to get on a plane and go somewhere terrifying and *become* someone new.
Where her anxiety didn't win.
I let the phone drop to the bed. It bounces once on the silk sheets and lies there, screen up, Fork app still open, Return button still waiting.
"Five more minutes," I whisper. "Just five more minutes."
Getting dressed should not be this hard.

I've been getting dressed for twenty-two years. It's one of the few things I'm reliably competent at — pants on, shirt on, shoes on, out the door. But this Avery's closet is not a closet. It's a museum of couture, and I am an uncredentialed tourist.

The closet is a walk-in — of course it's a walk-in — and it's organized by color, which would be helpful if I had any idea what any of these pieces were. There are fabrics I can't name. Structural things with boning. A jacket that appears to be made entirely of origami-level pleating. Shoes arranged on shelves like artifacts: stilettos, mules, boots with architectural heels, flats that probably cost more than my monthly rent.
I touch a sleeve — raw silk, butter-soft — and pull my hand back like I've been caught stealing.
The irony is not lost on me. I *am* stealing. I'm stealing this life, this wardrobe, this body that knows how to walk in heels I've never attempted. The least I can do is dress it properly.

Twenty minutes of escalating panic later, I find it: a simple black dress, knee-length, with a neckline that's high enough to feel like armor and sleeves that cover the nervous way I'm clutching my own arms. The shoes take longer — every pair in here seems designed for a woman who learned to walk on a tightrope — but I eventually find black pointed-toe heels with a manageable block heel. I can walk in these. Probably. Hopefully.
I step in front of the full-length mirror near the closet door, and the air leaves my lungs.
I look *good*.

Not different — not made-up, not costumed, not like I'm playing dress-up in someone else's skin. I look like… me. But the version I've always kept locked in a room at the back of my mind, the one who gets off the plane in a city where she knows no one and figures it out. The one whose jaw doesn't drop when she sees her own reflection. The one who doesn't apologize.

The dress fits perfectly. My hair falls over my shoulders in a way that makes sense now, framing the strong jawline and the shaped eyebrows and the confident set of my mouth. The heels add just enough height that I can see the city differently — or maybe that's just Paris, reshaping everything it touches.
I look like the woman I wished I could be.

The thought should feel good. It doesn't. It feels like standing on the edge of something very tall, looking down at the person I actually am, and realizing the distance between us is measured in more than kilometers.
I grab a coat — a long wool thing, camel-colored, that I found hanging by the door — and I check the phone again. The gallery opens at seven. It's 6:43. Google Maps says it's an eleven-minute walk.
*Gallery opening tonight — J will be there.*
Who is J?
I don't have time to find out. I shove the phone in my coat pocket, take a breath that tastes like expensive shampoo and fear, and open the front door.
The air hits me like a person.

Rain and cigarette smoke and something underneath — old stone and expensive perfume and the particular green smell of a river that's been flowing through a city for centuries. The evening is cool and damp, the kind of cold that doesn't bite but whispers, pressing against my bare legs and the open collar of my coat. I shiver, and it's not just the temperature.
I walk.

Google Maps guides me through streets that feel like movie sets — too beautiful, too specific, too *real* for someone who learned geography from textbooks. A café with red awnings and men in scarves smoking at small tables. A flower shop spilling buckets of roses onto the sidewalk. A woman walking a tiny dog that looks like it was bred specifically to fit in a handbag.

My heels click on the cobblestones — *click, click, click* — a metronome keeping time with my racing heart. Every step feels borrowed. Every person who glances at me sees the Avery Chen who lives here, the one who belongs on these streets, and I smile back at them like I know what I'm doing, and the smile feels like a lie I'm telling with someone else's mouth.
I cross a bridge over the Seine, and I have to stop.

The river is gunmetal grey under the evening sky, and the lights are starting to come on along the banks — warm yellow, romantic and cinematic, the kind of lighting that makes you understand why people write songs about this city. My heels are a little unsteady on the bridge's stone surface, and I grip the railing with fingers that are starting to feel the cold, and I look out at the water, and for one moment — one small, still moment — the anxiety quiets.
I'm in Paris.
I'm in *Paris*.

And then the moment passes, because Google Maps is telling me to turn left, and I have a party to attend, and I don't know anyone, and I'm wearing a dead woman's confidence and hoping nobody notices it doesn't fit.

The gallery is on a narrow street off the Rue du Faubourg Saint-Honoré. I know this because I've been staring at my phone for the last three blocks, navigating like a tourist, which is exactly what I am — a tourist in my own life, a fraud in borrowed couture, a glitch wearing someone else's skin.

The gallery has a glass front, warm light spilling out onto the wet sidewalk. People cluster at the entrance — angular women in black, men in blazers, the particular species of beautiful human that seems to exist only at art openings in European capitals. I slow my pace, buying time, trying to remember how the Avery Chen who lives here would walk into a room.
She wouldn't hesitate. She wouldn't stand on the street like a lost child. She would just — go.
So I go.
And that's when I see him.

He's standing at the gallery entrance, half-turned toward the door, half-turned toward the street. Tall. Gray-eyed. Sharp jaw that catches the light like architecture. White shirt, sleeves rolled to the elbow, and he's holding a glass of something amber that glows in the warm light from inside.
He looks right at me.

Not past me. Not near me. *At* me. And something in his expression — I can't decode it, I don't have the cipher — makes my heart stop. Recognition? Curiosity? Something harder, something with edges, something that feels like standing in front of a locked door you didn't know existed and realizing you've been carrying the key the whole time?
He doesn't smile. He doesn't look away. He just *looks*, steady and unhurried, like he's reading a book he's not sure he wants to finish but can't put down.

My heels have stopped clicking. I've stopped walking. The Paris evening rushes around me — laughter from inside, rain starting to spot my coat, the smell of wet stone and the taste of lipstick I didn't put on, bitten off on the walk here — and I'm standing frozen on the sidewalk, staring at a man whose name I don't know.
But I know his face.

Not from this life — not from this phone, not from these 47,000 followers, not from the Sorbonne Girls group chat or the dossier for Maison Deveraux. From somewhere else. Somewhere deeper. Somewhere that makes my bones vibrate and my breath come shallow and my hands curl inside my coat pockets like they're trying to hold onto something that isn't there.
He's still looking at me.

And I don't know who he is, but I know — with a certainty that settles into the space behind my ribs like a stone dropping into still water — that he's already reading me like a book I haven't finished writing. Like he's flipped to the last page and come back to watch me stumble toward the ending I don't know yet.
The rain picks up.
He doesn't move.
Neither do I.
